investor_personal_information.graduate_school: University of Michigan, Ross School of Business

employment_and_retirement_information.nature_of_business: Manufacturing and distribution of industrial HVAC components for commercial buildings (offices, hospitals, data centers, and light industrial facilities).
employment_and_retirement_information.position_and_duties: Senior Operations Manager – I oversee day-to-day plant operations at MidWest Thermal Components, Inc. in Columbus, Ohio. My responsibilities include managing a team of approximately 45 employees (line supervisors, maintenance techs, and logistics coordinators), setting monthly production targets based on sales forecasts, and coordinating with our finance team on budgeting and capital expenditures. I am responsible for reviewing weekly variance reports (labor, materials, and overhead), approving purchase orders over $5,000, and participating in quarterly strategic planning meetings with the COO and CFO. I also lead continuous improvement projects under our lean manufacturing initiative, such as reducing scrap rates on our custom air-handling units and renegotiating shipping contracts to lower freight costs.
employment_and_retirement_information.other_prior_occupations_or_duties_during: From 2019 to 2022, I served as Operations Project Manager at the same company, MidWest Thermal Components, Inc. In that role, I managed cross-functional projects such as consolidating two smaller warehouses into our current 120,000 sq. ft. distribution center and implementing a new inventory management system (NetSuite). I was responsible for vendor selection, negotiating implementation fees, coordinating training sessions for warehouse staff, and tracking project budgets against actuals. Prior to that, from 2017 to 2019, I worked as a Manufacturing Engineer at Buckeye Industrial Systems in Dayton, Ohio. My duties included designing and optimizing assembly line layouts, conducting time studies, and preparing cost-justification analyses for equipment upgrades. During that period, I also occasionally assisted the sales team with technical presentations to large industrial clients, explaining how process improvements could lower their total cost of ownership.

investment_history.investments_made_during_the_past_five_ye:
2019 – Opened a taxable brokerage account at Vanguard and purchased a diversified mix of low-cost index mutual funds (approximately 70% U.S. total stock market, 20% international developed markets, and 10% U.S. total bond market). Initial investment was approximately $15,000, funded from accumulated savings and an annual bonus.

2020 – Increased contributions to my employer-sponsored 401(k) plan with Fidelity, allocating funds primarily to target-date retirement funds (2050 target-date fund) and a small portion to a U.S. bond index fund. Total contributions (employee plus employer match) for 2020 were approximately $18,500.

2020 – During the COVID-19 market downturn (March–May 2020), made additional lump-sum investments of approximately $8,000 into my existing Vanguard index funds, focusing on the total U.S. stock market index and international index, to take advantage of lower valuations.

2021 – Purchased individual shares of three large-cap U.S. companies (Apple, Johnson & Johnson, and Procter & Gamble) through the same Vanguard brokerage account, primarily to gain more familiarity with individual stock analysis. Total amount invested in these three positions was approximately $6,500 spread over several months.

2021 – Opened a Roth IRA at Vanguard and contributed the annual maximum for that year ($6,000), invested entirely in a broad-based U.S. stock index fund. I have continued to contribute annually, but the initial 2021 contribution is included here for completeness of recent history.

2022 – Participated in my employer’s Employee Stock Purchase Plan (ESPP), purchasing shares of MidWest Thermal Components’ parent company (a publicly traded industrial conglomerate) at a 10% discount through payroll deductions. Total annual purchase amount was approximately $3,200.

2022 – Invested approximately $5,000 into a low-cost real estate investment trust (REIT) index fund within my brokerage account to gain limited exposure to commercial real estate without direct property ownership.

2023 – Increased 401(k) contributions again to approach the IRS annual limit, resulting in total contributions (including employer match) of approximately $22,000 for the year, allocated primarily to a 2050 target-date fund and a U.S. stock index fund.

2023 – Invested $4,000 in a short-term U.S. Treasury bond ETF in my brokerage account as a relatively conservative holding for funds I expect to need within 3–5 years, such as my children’s future education expenses.

2024 – Continued regular monthly contributions to both my Vanguard brokerage account and Roth IRA (projected total contributions for 2024 of approximately $10,000 to taxable brokerage and $7,000 to Roth IRA, subject to IRS limits). The allocations remain primarily in diversified index funds (U.S. total market, international, and bonds) with a small continued position in the individual stocks noted above.

investment_knowledge_and_representation.basis_for_answer_to_4a:
Over the past 15+ years, I have gradually built up both practical and educational experience in personal finance and investing. Professionally, as a Senior Operations Manager, I work closely with our finance department and review monthly and quarterly financial reports, which has helped me become comfortable with concepts such as cash flow, capital budgeting, depreciation, and cost of capital. I routinely analyze project proposals using basic discounted cash flow techniques and payback period calculations, so I am familiar with evaluating potential returns and risks in a structured way.

On a personal level, I have been actively managing my own investments since around 2009, starting with my 401(k) plan and then expanding into IRAs and a taxable brokerage account. I read several foundational investing books, including "The Bogleheads’ Guide to Investing" by Larimore, Lindauer, and LeBoeuf, and "A Random Walk Down Wall Street" by Burton Malkiel, which strongly influenced my preference for diversified, low-cost index funds over speculative trading. I regularly follow reputable financial news sources such as The Wall Street Journal, Morningstar, and the investing sections of major newspapers, and I consult objective resources like the Bogleheads.org wiki when making allocation decisions.

In practice, I maintain a written investment policy statement for my household that outlines our target asset allocation (currently 80% stocks and 20% bonds, with about 30% of the stock allocation in international markets), our rebalancing thresholds, and guidelines for handling market volatility. I have lived through and continued investing during major market downturns (notably the 2008–2009 financial crisis and the 2020 COVID-19 downturn). During those periods, I did not panic-sell; instead, I increased contributions when feasible and rebalanced toward my target allocation, which reinforced my understanding of risk, volatility, and long-term returns.

Although I do not consider myself a professional investor, I am comfortable reading and interpreting basic financial statements, understanding how different asset classes (stocks, bonds, and real estate investment trusts) behave, and assessing whether a particular investment aligns with my risk tolerance, time horizon, and overall financial plan. I understand that private or less-liquid investments generally carry higher risk, may be difficult to sell quickly, and should only constitute a limited portion of my overall portfolio. This combination of formal education (MBA with a finance focus), day-to-day exposure to financial decision-making at work, and more than a decade of real-world investing with my own money forms the basis for my belief that I have sufficient knowledge and experience to evaluate the merits and risks of this investment opportunity without relying on a purchaser representative.
